Show work ›
Here's the mental model I'd use for "pillars, parents, subtasks" around Availability and Operational
Resilience at MongoDB.
1. Top-level hierarchy
Company-level: MongoDB Operational Excellence
• Foundational properties: Security, Durability, Availability, Performance (SDAP). 1
• OpEx pillars: Quality, Velocity, Minimizing toil, Exposing risk. '
Program-level: Availability Program (R&D-wide)
• Output goal:
> Fewer than 11% of top-100 Atlas org clusters have a monthly read-write outage; no more than
1% of outages last > 4 hours (by availability SLI). 2 2
• Focus: real availability of Atlas data plane for top-100 customers (where rare, pathological failures
show up). 2
• Organized into pillars, each with a DRI and its own input goal/KPl. 2 3
Product-level: 9.0 "Operational Resilience" pillar
• In the MongoDB 9.0 narrative, "Operational Resilience" is one of five non-negotiable areas
(alongside All Clusters Sharded, Upgrade Experience, Disaggregated Storage, etc.).
4 5
• It focuses on workload management + self-protection under stress (queueing, backpressure,
operation rejection/prioritization) and fleet health telemetry that backs the Availability Program's
goals. 4
These three layers are aligned but distinct: OpEx → Availability Program → Operational Resilience /
Workload Management workstreams.
2. Availability Program pillars (input-goal view)
The Availability Program explicitly defines one input goal per pillar for FY26.
3 Those pillars are:
1. Detect outages (Detect pillar)
2. Iterate on outages (Iterate pillar)
3. Diagnose
4. Prevent outage in Server
5. Prevent outage in Cloud
6. Remediate outage
7. End-to-end Availability Reporting
Below is how they fit together and what they actually do.
